title: Tagine of chicken with preserved lemon and olives
subtitle: Moroccan braised chicken with turmeric, olives, and preserved lemon.
excerpt: The preserved lemon and olive tagine is probably the most famous Moroccan chicken dish outside Morocco: bone-in thighs braised in a spiced onion sauce with olives and strips of preserved lemon rind added towards the end. The preserved lemon adds a salted, fermented citrus note that nothing else replicates. One pan, one hour.
sourceNotes: The canonical Moroccan chicken tagine, documented throughout twentieth-century Moroccan culinary writing and widely attributed to the cuisine of Marrakech and the Moroccan culinary canon as codified by writers including Fatéma Hal. Preserved lemons (citrons confits) are a Moroccan pantry staple; the skin only is used, rinsed and sliced thin. Good deli-quality preserved lemons are now available in most UK supermarkets.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tagine keeps well in the fridge for two days and reheats gently in a covered pan.

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-based pan with a tight-fitting lid. Wide enough to hold the chicken pieces without stacking.

content.text: The preserved lemon gives this tagine its defining character. It is not a lemon flavour in the way that fresh lemon juice is bright and sharp; preserved lemon is fermented, salted, and complex, with a bitterness and depth that transforms the sauce around it. Only the rind is used (the flesh is too salty and mushy), rinsed and cut into strips, added in the final fifteen minutes so it warms through without losing its texture.

The olive choice matters. Green olives are traditional, and ideally cracked or unpitted rather than the pitted jarred variety, which can be rubbery. A good Castelvetrano or similar large green olive works well. The olives also go in towards the end, so they warm through without losing their bite.

Preserved lemons are available in larger supermarkets and Middle Eastern or North African shops. One lemon is enough for this dish. Rinse it well under cold water, cut it in quarters, scrape out the flesh and discard it, and slice the rind into thin strips.

items.prepNote: unpitted or pitted large green olives

content.text: You will also need 1 preserved lemon. Rinse it under cold water, discard the flesh, and cut the rind into thin strips. Preserved lemons are available in most large supermarkets and North African or Middle Eastern shops.

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs with salt and pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy pan over medium-high heat. Working in batches, place the chicken skin-side down and cook for six to seven minutes until the skin is deep golden. Turn and cook for two minutes on the flesh side. Transfer to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} sliced onions to the pan and cook for ten minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened. Add the {{garlic}} garlic, turmeric, cumin, and ginger, and cook for one minute.
Return the chicken to the pan, skin-side up. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with a lid slightly ajar, and cook for twenty-five minutes.
Add the olives and preserved lemon

Add {{olives-green}} of green olives and the preserved lemon rind strips to the pan. Remove the lid and cook for a further fifteen minutes until the sauce has reduced and the chicken is cooked through. Taste the sauce — the preserved lemon is salty, so you may not need to add much extra salt.
Scatter the chopped coriander over the top. Serve with couscous or bread.

items.cause: The preserved lemon rind was not rinsed, or too much salt was added at the seasoning stage.
items.fix: Always rinse preserved lemon under cold water before using. Season the chicken lightly before browning and taste the sauce at the end before adjusting.

items.cause: Lid was left on for too long.
items.fix: Remove the lid and cook over medium heat for five to ten minutes until reduced to a glossy consistency.
Substitute the zest of one lemon and a tablespoon of lemon juice. The flavour is brighter and less complex, but the dish still works.

content.text: The chicken with preserved lemon and olives tagine is the most widely recognised Moroccan dish outside Morocco. It is the dish that appears in cookery books from Elizabeth David onwards, and the one that most reliably introduces preserved lemons to British cooks who have not used them before. Within Morocco it is a home-cooking standard, made by feel and without a recipe, with adjustments for the saltiness of the particular lemons used and the brininess of the olives to hand. The version that travels well is the one made simply, without saffron or other additions that require sourcing: which is this one.
